texts.orig: ABSTRACT: A novel industrial lithium-containing wastewater depth concentrating process integrating reverse osmosis (RO) and electrodialysis (ED) into a system is presented. A systematic analytical study was accomplished to optimize the studied parameters and minimize the energy consumption. The tested parameters were as follows: RO recovery by adding pressure, ED voltage drop, the concentration of RO retentate in ED feed solution, ED volume ratio, and ED operating mode. By using RO retentate instead of initial wastewater in the ED process, water energy consumption was reduced by 3.41 times from 26.67 to 7.81 kW h/m 3 , while optimizing the RO retentate concentration for the ED feed solution reduced the cost to 0.47 $/kg. The results showed that RO is crucial to preconcentrate lithium salt and save energy. Furthermore, the fi nal LiCl concentration can approach as high as 87.09 g/L with the secondary ED process ( V d : V c = 3:1), while the energy consumption can be saved as 7.71 kW h/m 3 when the experiments

texts.text: ■ INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lithium has attracted ever-growing levels of scientists ' interest with the increasing demand for a modern electronic revolution. In comparison with other metals, its higher electrochemical standard potential of 3.04 V and lower density of 0.534 g/cm 3 stand out. 1 About 35% of global lithium resources are used for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), and the demand for lithium will increase to approximately 66% by 2025. 2,3 Meanwhile, lithium and its related products have played a signi fi cant role in pharmaceuticals, lubricants, aluminum production, nuclear industry and synthetic rubbers. 4 -7 Lithium has industrially emerged mainly as lithium carbonate, lithium chloride, and lithium hydroxide. In particular, lithium carbonate has emerged as a vital lithium industrial product in the basic raw materials for the preparation of lithium metal and secondary lithium. 8 Therefore, the exploration of accurate and e ff ective methods for separation, extraction, and puri fi cation of lithium from all viable resources is essential.

texts.orig: Currently, the global resources of lithium are mainly classi fi ed as the primary resources and secondary resources. 8 -10 For primary resources, lithium is mostly derived from clays, minerals, salt lakes, and seawater. 1 However, it is hard to meet the demand of our rapid development of industry and society only relying on lithium extraction from primary

texts.label: text
texts.orig: resources. Therefore, the rising attention for lithium calls for processing all viable resources especially the secondary resources of spent LIBs. According to the report, about 5 -7 wt % of lithium is deposited in spent LIBs. 11 In particular, during the operation of spent LIBs, the lithium salt is always discharged as wastewater, which causes a signi fi cant waste of lithium resources. 12 Consequently, it is necessary to extract lithium salt from the potential resources of industrial lithiumcontaining wastewater. Generally speaking, Li2CO3 can be precipitated out through carbonation by addition of Na2CO3 into the lithium-containing wastewater, and therefore, the methods of lithium salt concentrating using the lithiumcontaining wastewater are essential. Traditionally, multistage fl ash, multiple e ff ect distillation, and thermal technology can be used for lithium salts concentrating, but the drawbacks of labor intensity and high energy consumption limit their industrialscale application. Therefore, an economical and e ff ective concentrating method can be developed to substitute the present evaporation technology.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane technology for wastewater treatment has increased in popularity in recent years. Concentrating via reverse osmosis (RO) is a separation process for making highsaline brine from wastewater. 13 Water molecules will migrate through RO membranes under an appropriate pressure exerted above wastewater, and salts are concentrated subsequently. 14 Compared to the thermal technology and multistage fl ash distillation, the popularity of RO comes from the advantages of saving energy consumption, high water recovery e ffi ciency, fl exibility, and small plant size. 15 Although RO has become an e ff ective method for wastewater recovery, further concentrating of high-saline brine still needs to be explored to minimize the processing cost. Electrodialysis (ED), an industrially established concentration process, has been widely applied in industrial wastewater reclamation, seawater desalination, and production of potable water from brackish water. 16 -19 Zhou et al. reported the concentrating of lithium salt from lithium sulfate solution through ED using di ff erent operating modes. 20 Simultaneously, ED has been reported to be a great advantage of achieving high brine concentration (total dissolved solids, TDS, of 150 -200 g/L), and RO is suitable for the preconcentrating of wastewater with the superiority of relatively lower energy consumption. 21 Oren et al. have conducted ED for concentrating brackish RO brine to the TDS of 10%, 22 while in another study, Jiang et al. treated ROconcentrated brine and achieved a coarse salt production by using a multistage ED process. 23 As the combination of RO and ED for the processing of lithium-containing wastewater is a potential method, it is essential to evaluate the feasibility of this process for recycling lithium resources from wastewater.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this investigation, a nearly zero discharge process for industrial lithium-containing wastewater depth concentrating was proposed. As shown in Figure 1, the industrial lithiumcontaining wastewater was primary puri fi ed by cation-exchange resins to treat Ca 2+ and Mg 2+ for avoiding membrane scaling issues in the RO process. RO is explored as a preconcentrating step to recover lithium salts from industrial lithium-containing wastewater. Subsequently, a lab-scale ED process for concentrating the RO retentate was carried out not only to ensure the feasibility of the system but also to con fi rm if the desalted solution from the ED process can be reused for the RO process. The concentrated solution from the ED process, which enriched LiCl and NaCl, can be used to produce Li2CO3 by addition of Na2CO3. 24 The objective of this work is to investigate the e ff ect of operating pressure on the RO process. Simultaneously, the e ff ects of the parameters (e.g., voltage drop, RO retentate concentration, operation mode, and volume ratio) on the ED process were evaluated in terms of concentration factor, average fl ux of Li + , water transport, and energy consumption. Furthermore, an industrial fl owsheet for the integrated RO -ED process was studied by concentration factor and water recovery rate as well. This research hopefully provides a reference for the comprehensive utilization of membrane technologies on lithium-containing wastewater recovery.

texts.orig: Enrichment of Lithium Chloride by the Hybrid RO -ED Process. The unit of the RO process consisted of a membrane cell, a rotameter, a manometer, a rotary vane pump, a retentate tank, and a permeate tank, similar to the previous publication. 26,27 The fi ltration area of the RO membrane was 0.32 m 2 , and the retentate tank was fi lled with lithium-containing wastewater from the cation-exchange column e ffl uent. Before the experiment, the RO membrane was circulated by 2% citric acid solution for 30 min and conditioned by permeating deionized water at an operational pressure of 10 bar. Then, the lithium-containing wastewater will be concentrated in the retentate tank as the experiment progresses, and the CH-93 cation-exchange resins can be washed by the RO permeate after the experiment.

In detail, the ED membrane stack was composed of the following units: (1) ED membrane stack, in which titanium is coated with ruthenium (anode) and graphite (cathode); (2) electrode tank feed with 3% Na2SO4 as the electrode rinse solution; (3) dilute tank and concentrated tank feed with solution from the RO retentate in the initial, respectively; and (4) a CV/CC regulated power supply of WYL3010S (from Hangzhou Siling Electrical Instrument Ltd., China). Each unit was composed of a dilute compartment and a concentrated compartment. Furthermore, two pieces of electrode membranes (C with e ff ective surface area of 189 cm 2 ) were installed adjacent to the electrodes for avoiding the SO 4 2 -and Na + leak from the electrode compartment to the concentrated compartment. Before the supply of the CV/CC regulated power supply, the solution in each tanks circulated for 30 min to eliminate the gas bubbles on the surface of the membranes. 25 To achieve lithium salt with a high concentration, two di ff erent operation modes during the ED process were proposed. A partial cyclic operation mode refers to the concentrated solution that was circulated throughout the experiment, while the dilute solution was replaced by a new feed after each batch of the experiment. Another operation mode is a high volume ratio (1:1, 2:1, and 3:1) between the dilute solution and concentrated solution in the multistage concentrating process.

texts.orig: Comparison of Lithium Recovery through RO and ED Processes. E ff ect of Voltage Drop during the ED Process Using Partial Cyclic Operation Mode. Voltage drop is a signi fi cant parameter a ff ecting the migration rate of ions and average fl ux during the ED process. According to the literature, the voltage drop across one unit of ED membrane stack is in the range 0.5 -2.5 V. 20 In the following experiment, the voltage drops of 6 and 8 V were used to compare the performance of the ED process. The initial volume of the dilute solution and concentrated solution was 300 and 300 mL, respectively; the dilute solution was replaced by a new feed with a volume of 300 mL after each batch of the experiment. When the conductivity of dilute solution decreased to 1 mS/cm, each batch of the experiment was stopped.

texts.orig: Figure 4b illustrates the speci fi c energy consumption between the RO and ED process. It can be seen from Figure 4b that the speci fi c energy consumption observed for the RO process slightly varied from 7.58 to 7.81 kW h/m 3 as result of the operating pressure changing from 15 to 20 bar. The observed unimportant increase is caused by the higher driving force supplied in terms of the operating pressure. Conversely, for the ED process, the energy consumption signi fi cantly increased during ED operation from 16.89 to 26.67 kW h/m 3 with the voltage drop of 6 and 8 V, respectively. It can be inferred that more energy will be required to overcome the ionic migration at the higher voltage drop. 34 Compared to the RO process, when the operating pressure is 15 bar, the energy consumption increased by 2.23 times for a voltage drop of 6 V in the ED process. Simultaneously, when the operating pressure increased up to 20 bar, the energy consumption accordingly increased by 3.41 times for a voltage drop of 8 V in the ED process. As a consequence, it is possible to a ffi rm that the RO, in terms of speci fi c energy consumption, is a more e ffi cient separation process than the traditional ED technology, and the observations suggest that the preliminary concentrating lithium-containing wastewater by RO process can be considered as a techno-economic procedure.

texts.orig: ED Process Using RO Retentate as Feed. E ff ect of RO Retentate Concentration on the ED Process. Generally, the initial concentration of the feed solution is a signi fi cant operational factor during the ED process, since it may determine water transport, average fl ux, current e ffi ciency, and energy consumption. In the following experiment, di ff erent solutions with the conductivities of 50, 55, 60, and 65 mS/cm were tested at a voltage drop of 8 V. The initial volume of the concentrated solution and dilute solution was the same (300 mL). Accordingly, below a certain value of conductivity for the dilute solution (1 mS/cm), the experiment was stopped.

texts.orig: Figure 5a shows ED results of RO retentate whose initial concentration was in the range from 55 to 65 mS/cm at a voltage drop of 8 V. The experimental results indicate that the current exhibits a downtrend throughout the experiment. This is because the conductivity of the dilute compartment decreases with the elapse of time; thus, the electrical resistance of the ED membrane stack increases consistently. Furthermore, a higher concentration of dilute solution can extend the experimental time and enhance the current. This phenomenon can be also explained in terms of higher LiCl concentration that reduces the electrical resistance of the ED membrane stack e ff ectively. 35 As shown in Figure 5b, the conductivity of the concentrated solution increases within an experimental time of 25 min. At the latter stage of the experiment, the curves display a relatively slow upward trend, which is due to the high concentration-gradient between the concentrated compartment and dilute compartment. 36

texts.orig: Figure 5d illustrates the e ff ect of the initial concentration of RO retentate solution on the energy consumption and current e ffi ciency. The current e ffi ciency results show an apparent maximum when the conductivity of RO retentate is 55 mS/cm. The energy consumption steadily increases from 36.59 to 55.31 kW h/m 3 as the conductivities of the initial dilute and concentrated solution rise from 50 to 65 mS/cm. The increasing energy consumption of the ED stack in di ff erent concentrations of solutions is caused by the overcoming of electrical resistance. 37 Therefore, from the point of view of current e ffi ciency values, the RO retentate conductivity value of 55 mS/cm can be considered as optimal for a highest current e ffi ciency of 87.88%.

texts.orig: Comparison with RO Retentate Concentration and Process Economics. In the RO -ED integration process, the e ff ect of RO retentate concentration on the ED process cost is shown in Table 3. The process cost is calculated according to ref 38. The total process comprised the total investment cost and total energy cost. 39 The membrane stack cost was calculated by membrane cost multiplied by a coe ffi cient of 1.5, and peripheral equipment cost was calculated by membrane stack cost multiplied by the coe ffi cient of 1.5.
texts.text: Comparison with RO Retentate Concentration and Process Economics. In the RO -ED integration process, the e ff ect of RO retentate concentration on the ED process cost is shown in Table 3. The process cost is calculated according to ref 38. The total process comprised the total investment cost and total energy cost. 39 The membrane stack cost was calculated by membrane cost multiplied by a coe ffi cient of 1.5, and peripheral equipment cost was calculated by membrane stack cost multiplied by the coe ffi cient of 1.5.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As represented in Table 3, the total process cost exhibits a downtrend as the conductivity of RO retentate increases. For an RO retentate value of 65 mS/cm, the process cost is estimated around 0.47 $/kg. The process capacity exhibits an upward trend over the experiments, and the ED process cost would be decreased if industrial-scale setup is employed. Therefore, it can be concluded that the increasing RO retentate concentration is crucial for the reduction of the process cost of ED.

texts.orig: As illustrated in Figure 6a, the current decreases with the increase of the volume between the concentrated compartment and dilute compartment. This is due to the migration of Li + and Cl -from the dilute compartment to the concentrated compartment; thus, the membrane resistance gradually rises. Simultaneously, the current is proportional to the volume ratio over the experiment. Figure 6b shows that the conductivity of the concentrated solution increases consistently during the experiment with the volume ratio of 1:1, 2:1, and 3:1, respectively. At the latter stage of the experiment, the conductivity of the concentrated solution tends to be stable, which is due to the occurrence of forward osmosis between the dilute compartment and concentrated compartment. In detail, water is extracted from the dilute solution into the concentrated solution by the osmotic pressure di ff erence, thus causing a decrease in the conductivity of the concentrated solution. 40 Furthermore, a higher volume ratio is prone to achieve a higher LiCl concentration, even though a part of the water will migrate into the concentrated compartment.

texts.orig: Figure 7c shows how volume ratio a ff ects the water transport and average fl ux of Li + during the secondary ED process. The Li + fl ux in the second stage of concentrating is higher than that in the fi rst stage of concentrating, which is because of the lower stack resistance in the second stage of concentrating compared to the fi rst stage of concentrating. Meanwhile, the highest water transport can be obtained in the second stage of concentrating with volume ratio of 3:1. The main reason is that the phenomenon of electro-osmosis will be aggravated at a high salt concentrating process. From Figure 7d, it can be further observed that the energy consumption has been divided into two regions according to the conductivity. When the conductivity of the concentrated compartment increases almost linearly with time, we will call this region 1. Region 2 refers to the steady areas in Figure 7b. As can be observed, while experiments during the second stage of concentrating are stopped in region 2, it will consume more energy at 12.25 kW h/m 3 with the volume ratio of 1:1 and 7.71 kW h/m 3 with the volume ratio of 3:1  a speci fi c energy consumption increase of 14.85% and 13.52%, respectively. This is expected since the concentration di ff erence between the dilute compartment and concentrated compartment enhanced in region 2; thus, a greater amount of electrical energy will be transformed into joules of heat. 41 Furthermore, with the addition of the concentrating stage at a volume ratio of 3:1, the total energy consumption increases from 32.63 to 82.51 kW h/m 3 , and the current e ffi ciency decreases from 73.86% to 58.26%, respectively.

texts.orig: volume ratio, and operation mode were systematically studied in the ED process. The RO process exhibited an excellent separation performance for the preconcentrating of lithium salt, allowing a relatively low energy consumption of 7.58 kW h/m 3 compared to the ED process. The increment of RO retentate concentration reduced the process cost to 0.47 $/kg e ff ectively. Moreover, the supplement of the ED process can depth concentrate lithium salt at the volume ratio of 3:1, and simultaneously, the secondary ED concentrating is more competitive from the total concentration factor of 12.32. During the secondary ED process, the experiment was stopped in region 1 with a volume ratio of 3:1 which can reduce the energy consumption of 7.71 kW h/m 3 . The system proposed in this paper not only achieved energy savings but also is feasible for lithium salt concentrating, which indicated a high potential in industrial applications.
